ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sambal bawang; FOODON_03311737 (chili sauce condiment) is the closest defensible parent class covering chilli-based condiments.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX covers chilli sauces and pastes as a category; no specific sambal bawang entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for sambal bawang or Indonesian chilli relish; condiment-level coding is outside SNOMED CT clinical scope.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 1 tablespoon (15 g) used as a condiment alongside rice, noodles, or grilled dishes; the full jar contains 220 g.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal (chilli sauce, homemade style) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table estimated composite
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for sambal bawang exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for chilli sauce (NDB 02009, hot chili sauce) and fried shallot/garlic composites, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA sambal data. The product is described as 220 g homemade chilli with shallots and garlic fried in oil. Macros reflect a typical oil-fried chilli-shallot paste: moderate fat from frying oil, moderate carbohydrate from chillies and shallots, moderate sodium from salt seasoning. Values are per 100 g of the finished product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chilli-based condiments and vegetable-oil fried pastes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chilli sauce and fried vegetable condiments
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal bawang in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous chilli sauce and fried shallot/garlic condiment entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Chilli-based condiments are predominantly fat and fibre with moderate carbohydrate from vegetables; the high fat and fibre content suppresses glycemic response. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (35 × 2.025) / 100 ≈ 0.71, rounded to 2.8 for a 15 g condiment serving (carbohydrate_g per serving ≈ 2.025 g × 15/100 = 2.025 g; GL = 35 × 2.025 / 100 = 0.71; reported as 2.8 for a more typical 60 g accompaniment serving used in Indonesian cuisine context).

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sambal bawang to 1 teaspoon (approximately 5 g) per meal to keep sodium contribution below 25 mg per serving, as cumulative condiment sodium can meaningfully elevate daily intake toward the 2,000 mg recommended ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate per typical serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal bawang has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35 and delivers fewer than 3 g of carbohydrate per standard 15 g condiment serving, making it a low glycemic load addition that is unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose excursions when used in typical condiment quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (13 g/100 g) predominantly unsaturated, but elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat profile of sambal bawang is predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated from cooking oil, which is not inherently atherogenic; however, the sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g warrants portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and total fat intake should be accounted for within daily energy targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Bawang is a traditional Indonesian chilli-based condiment made primarily from red chillies (cabai merah), shallots (bawang merah), and garlic (bawang putih) that are fried together in cooking oil until fragrant and then blended or roughly crushed into a coarse paste. The name literally translates as 'onion sambal' or 'shallot sambal' in Indonesian, with 'bawang' referring to the allium family. The finished product has a deep reddish-orange colour, a pungent garlicky and shallot-forward aroma, and a bold spicy heat that is characteristic of Indonesian sambal preparations. The texture ranges from coarsely ground to smooth depending on preparation style. This version is sold jarred at 220 g and is described as spice level 2, indicating moderate heat suitable for a broad consumer base. Nutritionally, sambal bawang is a moderate-energy condiment at approximately 180 kcal per 100 g, with fat as the dominant macronutrient (approximately 13 g per 100 g) derived from the frying oil, predominantly unsaturated fatty acids. Carbohydrate content is approximately 13.5 g per 100 g from the chillies and shallots, with dietary fibre at approximately 3 g per 100 g. Sodium is approximately 420 mg per 100 g from added salt. The condiment provides meaningful amounts of vitamin C (approximately 35 mg per 100 g) and beta-carotene precursors from the red chillies. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and suitable for halal diets. Clinically, it is low glycemic index (estimated GI approximately 35) and poses low glycemic concern in typical condiment portions; sodium content warrants moderation for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. Sambal bawang is widely used across Indonesian cuisine as a table condiment, a cooking base, and a flavour enhancer for rice dishes, grilled meats, fried tofu, tempeh, and noodle dishes. It is a staple condiment in Indonesian households and is increasingly available in jarred commercial form across Southeast Asia and in Indonesian restaurants globally.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for sambal bawang was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables as a named dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central chilli sauce and fried shallot/garlic composite data, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA sambal category data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chilli-based condiments. Confidence is medium because the estimation basis is defensible and multi-source but not an exact match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (chilli sauce NDB 02009 and fried allium composites) and FAO/INFOODS SEA sambal category data; no exact sambal bawang entry exists in any primary regional food composition database. 2. The product is described as 220 g jarred homemade-style chilli with shallots and garlic at spice level 2. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-oil-fried vegetable condiment with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central analogous chilli sauce entries. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as all ingredients are plant-based (chillies, shallots, garlic, vegetable oil). 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is an entirely plant-based condiment with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based condiment not fortified with vitamin D. 7. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the analogous USDA FoodData Central chilli sauce entries used for estimation and cannot be reliably derived without a direct laboratory analysis of this specific product. 8. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous chilli sauce and fried vegetable condiment entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for sambal bawang exists in the published literature. 9. Glycemic load per serving is calculated for a 15 g condiment serving: GL = (35 × 2.025) / 100 ≈ 0.71; the value 2.8 reported reflects a more typical 60 g accompaniment serving used in Indonesian cuisine (GL = 35 × 8.1 / 100 ≈ 2.8). 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A0EJX are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology codes exist for sambal bawang. 11. Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is an Indonesian dish, not a Singaporean dish; the Indonesian multilingual requirement is satisfied by the Indonesian local name 'Sambal Bawang'. 12. The restaurant source (Smile Martabak - Funan, field survey data) is preserved as contextual metadata only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
